attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a painless intermittent swelling in his right groin that has been present for one year. The swelling is most noticeable when standing. His medical history includes a previously treated melanoma on his right leg and varicose vein surgery on his left leg. He has a 20 pack-year smoking history, drinks about three cans of beer a week and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a 2 cm compressible swelling located 5 cm inferior and lateral to the pubic tubercle. The swelling has an expansile cough impulse and disappears when supine. The right leg shows some superficial varicose veins extending up into his thigh and groin but is otherwise normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a classic description of a saphena varix, which is a dilated varicose swelling of the proximal long saphenous vein. The key features supporting saphena varix are the location below the inguinal ligament, reducibility when supine, presence of a cough impulse, and associated superficial varicose veins. Associated signs of venous insufficiency in the lower limbs, though not present in this case, can assist in making the diagnosis.

Inguinal hernia would typically present above the inguinal ligament, though it shares the features of reducibility and cough impulse. The location of this swelling makes an inguinal hernia unlikely.

Femoral hernia occurs in a similar location but typically does not reduce spontaneously due to the narrow, rigid nature of the femoral canal. Femoral hernias also carry a high risk of incarceration and require urgent surgical attention.

Hydrocele of the cord would present as a smooth, fluctuant swelling along the spermatic cord that transilluminates. It would not demonstrate a cough impulse or reduce when supine.

Enlarged inguinal lymph node would be firm, non-compressible, and would not demonstrate a cough impulse or reducibility. Lymphadenopathy would also prompt investigation for underlying causes such as infection or malignancy. With this patient's history of a melanoma excision from his right leg, the possibility of metastatic spread to an inguinal node should be considered, however, the physical signs do not support such a diagnosis.
learningPoints: A saphena varix is a dilated varicose swelling of the proximal long saphenous vein that presents as a groin lump.
Saphena varix can be distinguished from hernias by its location below the inguinal ligament and association with venous insufficiency.
Reducible groin lumps that disappear when supine are most likely to be either inguinal hernias or saphena varices.
references: Ravikanth R. Thrombosed Saphena Varix. J Med Ultrasound. 2019 Jan-Mar;27(1):54-55. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31031538/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
